narrative: As a Black male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on academic and background factors from past students. The model estimated a 48% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which crossed the threshold for rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting those predicted to pass with high confidence. Your profile includes solid school deciles of 7 in both Year 1 and Year 3—above the typical student's 5.7 and 5.5—along with a middle-income family background, an undergraduate GPA of 2.6 (below the average 3.21), an LSAT score of 28 (well under the average 36.7), and plans to attend full-time, which is the norm for over 90% of students. The decision hinged on three key factors, ranked by their influence on your specific prediction. Most significantly, your LSAT score pushed the model strongly toward predicting bar failure, as it stood out as a major concern compared to what succeeds for others. Counterbalancing that, your Year 3 school decile had the second-strongest positive influence, nudging the prediction toward passing by showing stronger late performance than average. Your Year 1 decile provided the third-most important boost in the same direction, reflecting early academic standing above the norm. While these school rankings helped, they weren't enough to offset the LSAT's dominant drag on the overall assessment. This mix placed you in a tougher spot than typical admitted students, whose stronger standardized scores often tip the scales despite similar academics. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above the average, as that's the clearest lever here—many students in your position have succeeded by targeting that improvement. Strengthening your application with recent achievements or bar prep evidence could also help in reapplying, showing the model your trajectory. You're clearly capable in school rankings, so channeling that into test prep might flip the prediction next time. Keep pushing; law school doors reopen for determined applicants like you.
